sentences.text: We arrived at night in Auschwitz.
The trucks unloaded us in long, dark barracks that lined a unpaved street.
And there seemed to be barracks like that everywhere.
We had absolutely no sense of orientation, where we were, how big that place was, and what is going to happen to us.
Not really.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have friends or family have received post cards that simply said that they were in a place that we could not find on any map.
And some of those now in that camp as well, which we found only later.
Men and-- were separated from women.
I was with my father.
And-- in one of these barracks, and my recollection is that one of the first acts was that we were tattooed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and received the number that we all carried.
My father, his number was next to mine.
And even though it didn't really hurt too much, it was a very frightening experience for a small boy.
It was-- it was something that I remember happened shortly afterwards.
But I can't tell you whether it was a few or hours or half a day later.
Yes.
We-- we were eventually sent to a so-called sauna for showers.
But at that time, especially children, if I recall, could keep some of their clothing because they could not so easily match children's clothing, provide clothing for children.
The exceptional state of-- of our condition was that we were kept as whole families.
We were not, as I started saying, greeted by Mengele, and selected right then and there.
Of course, we didn't know any of this until we learned later what is the normal procedure.

So we-- after a few days of having to stay in the same barracks, we were allowed to move around a little more during certain hours of the day, and to reunite family and meet my mother, and exchange experiences and find either relatives of friends that preceded us to this camp.
Birkenau was an enormous camp made out of literally hundreds of huge barracks that were originally designed as stables for horses.
There was-- they were made out of wood, the roof was held by roof trusses with a band of narrow windows at the top.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bunk beds lining the sides with a horizontal chimney that had two vertical chimneys at each end that was sometimes used for minimal heating in the bitter winter.
Oh, there were maybe 150 feet long, and there were several hundred people in each barrack.
There were-- the bunk beds were three layers high, and there were six people on each horizontal bunk bed.
And we were literally, when the camp was full, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sandwiched in.
And one person couldn't arbitrarily turn over.
They would have to-- the whole row would have to turn over.
And it was very, very compressed.
And to go to relieve yourself at night was extremely excruciating.
We couldn't leave the barrack.
There were buckets that quickly overflowed at the doorways.

And every night, they were stacked up near the door, and usually the clothing was stripped by others because there was a-- especially in the winter, there was such a shortage of clothing that a pair of socks or gloves was very highly valued.
The-- at the end of-- each camp was surrounded-- each compartment was surrounded by-- by wire, barbed wire.
And there was the street, as I said, in the middle of a row of barracks.
There were 30 barracks, roughly, to each camp.
The front of the camp had a small house which housed the German guards, and had a gate from which people came in and out.
There was only one gate to each camp.
And then the kitchen and the storage rooms was the next barrack.
And at the opposite end was a latrine that consisted of a-- of a concrete block with a bunch of holes in it.

And people were led-- each barrack was led to this latrine at specific hours [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to relieve themselves, and-- and similar barrack had just a long pipe with holes in it where people could wash occasionally.
Of course, it was all cold water.
No.
This-- this camp-- this particular camp, or segment of Birkenau, was purely devoted to the families from Theresienstadt.
So there were-- with the arrival of our transport, there were 10,000 inmates minus all the people who died in the mean-- in the three months since we arrived.
And people died daily.
There was a very heroic Zionist called Fredy Hirsch who was devoted to caring for children, and gave his life to that.

And he persuaded the SS to set aside one barrack which would serve as a place for children who could not work, could gather during the day, and somehow by-- through the help and instructions of a few chosen educators, could [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] forget a little bit where they-- where we were.
In-- real teaching was not allowed.
But we were taught songs and games, and a little bit of here and there.
Several hundred.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It took quite a bit of doing to persuade the Germans to allow this activity.
Whereas the grown-ups had assigned jobs-- some would be sent out for road work.
Others would work paving the street in-- in the camp, or building additional barracks.
Or when he was-- was sent somewhere for farm work.
And every day, these work groups would bring back dead comrades that would not be able to sustain the heavy work.
We had to also stand outside twice a day, once in the morning, once in the evening, to be counted.
And one of the privileges this educator, Herr Hirsch, received for the children, that we would be counted in the barrack rather than standing outside in the cold or-- or rain.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We-- my father was ill on and off.
And when he was not ill, as a doctor, he was assigned to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] look-- to-- to screen people for lice.
Lice carried typhus and other diseases, and because there was so much dirt, it-- and no-- lack of water for washing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were doing everything possible to kill the lice that were swarming over us.
And doctors were assigned to do that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When he was-- early on, I remember that he did not look well, and suffered a great deal.
And at that time, he was only 53 years old.
He went into the gas chambers later on, and I'll tell you about it in sequence.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In March, March 7, to be specific, of 1944, exactly six months after the first transport of 5,000 arrived there, they were taken away by truck to the next camp.
No.
The 5,000 people who arrived with the first transport.
We were the second transport.
The reason we all thought-- or the theory was that we were in this camp, children, old people, families together.
It was called [GERMAN], the family camp.
Is that the Germans were negotiating with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [GERMAN], the Red-- Red Cross, which was a Swiss volunteer organization.
To show them that the rumors that people are sent to gas chambers and only men and women that were capable of hard work were spared.
They were trying to dispel this-- this image, and wanted the Swiss Red Cross to visit the camp.
So they thought that if they kept us together, we would be a proof to them that they do not kill children and women.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Such a visit did occur in 1944 in Terezin, in Theresienstadt, at which time they literally cleaned up parts of the ghetto and in-- managed to fool the Red Cross, and believe that the Jews have a paradise in Terezin.
They finally decided that they did not want to bring them into Auschwitz.
And in March, they took the 5,000 men, women, and children into a adjacent camp.
They were there one night, and during the night they loaded them up in-- into trucks and took them away.
They actually just took them around the camp and into the gas chambers.
This Fredy Hirsch, that was the youth organizer, probably knew what was awaiting them, and he took some poison and died before he was sent to the gas chambers.
But--
In his late 30s.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He was a very charismatic person.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a frightening experience for us, the 5,000 people who were left behind.
And we did not all believe that they went into the gas chambers.
There was always this myth that they went to another camp.
And in fact, the day before-- the night before they were killed, they each had to fill out a post card-- which was the normal thing to do-- that was post-dated a whole month.
In other words, April something, to-- to send to some relative or friend in-- back home, saying we are well.
Do not worry.
Their signature.

[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] As time went by, we were more and more frightened because we believed that we will [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] have the same fate, that six months after our arrival, that we will all be gassed as well.
And by then, the gas chambers were in full production with transports arriving from Hungary and Poland and all over Europe nightly.
And the sky was glowing red from the crematoria, that often they had to also burn the bodies on open piles.
So the smell and the-- the smoke was pretty obvious.
Yet somehow, as a child, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we built-- as children, we built in some kind of a resistance, of not accepting it.
And this myth that it's not happening, even though it was less than half a kilometer away.
Sticks with me, that-- that [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was-- I was trying all this time not to take things in, to live day to day and not think ahead, what could happen, or could not happen.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the whole experience was to be as inconspicuous as possible.
Not to be too tall.
Not to be too small.
Not to be too slow.
Not to be too fast.
Not to have any color.
To be just blend into the background.
And that became particularly important later on.
Not to be selected.
Not-- if some-- a German came and said, who can-- who has a nice handwriting and wants to-- to help us paint or something like that.
Inevitably, those people were selected for some hard labor or clean toilets or something.
And there were many choices that later on one had to do.
Is should you go right, should you go left.
Should you stand with this crowd.
Should you stand with another.
And the manner of survival was complete series of coincidences and luck.
I don't believe that only the strong survived, or only the smart survived, or-- or only the strong or half-witted survived.
The-- this-- there were so many events, at least in my life, which were purely coincidental.
It's more a-- a feeling that I have now than going back and recalling specific events.
When the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was another transport that came from Terezin in May of 1944.
And that transport also came to our camp to take the place of the 5,000 that was gassed.
So we were once again completely full.
But this did not last very long.
Almost to the day six months after we arrived, or close to that period, there were rumors that the camp is being liquidated.
And we were very much afraid that we will all be gassed.
But then the order came that all the men that were capable of hard work should report for selection.
And that was done primarily by Josef Mengele, that would-- people would strip and had to walk in front of him, and he would decide whether they were good candidates for-- for work.
No, I was a child then.
So the camp started emptying out, first men and then women.
My mother volunteered for work.
My father couldn't because he was not only too old, but by that time he was really very ill, and was in a block, in a barrack, for sick people.
And I occasionally came to see him, and was shocked at his-- his condition and his resignation that he will not survive.
No.
Oh, some semblance of whatever we-- I mean, we never-- they never replenished.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But essentially, us children had our old clothes that we brought with us a year earlier.
Well, until then, my-- even though I did not see my parents daily, the feeling that we were all in the same camp did give children some support, some feeling of quasi security.
Of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of course, there was very little that we could decide or-- or make choices of our free will.

So it was, as I said, day to day matter of living through the day, and hoping for a little piece of bread with a-- with a square of margarine in the evening, which was the main food ration that we got.
Got some watered down black coffee in the morning, and a-- a watery soup at lunchtime, and that was all we would get.
So those were the kind of main events, because hunger by then was a gnawing, very-- very debilitating feeling all the time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as children, we-- I believe we got slightly better food.
But slightly better, I couldn't tell you now what that meant.
No.
At that time, we did not.
As the-- then-- as I said, then there were women who were selected for work, and shipped out to another camp in Birkenau for temporary storage or holding.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It must have been a very difficult decision, in retrospect, for mothers, whether they should abandon their children or face certain death by staying behind.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When all the able-bodied men and women were selected and left the camp, only small children, mothers with children, and sick people were left behind.
Elderly and sick people.

And I was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] suddenly notified-- and I don't remember how-- that a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the officers, SS officers, decided that it would be a good idea to have a large group of young boys chosen for lighter works in the camp, in the camp itself, in Birkenau-Auschwitz.
This was during the day, and we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] asked to strip in the same barrack that we were sort of during the day normally playing.
And had to-- I think we had to stand on a bench and he would look us over and decide sort of with a little whip whether we were OK or whether we should stay in the camp.
And this happened-- we had to do that twice.
First, he did it once.
And then either we were too many that he selected, went through it again.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was amongst those that were in the group that was immediately shipped out of the camp to another camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two camps over.
Which was the man's camp, [GERMAN].

And we were placed in barrack number 13, which also partly housed-- it was the punishment barrack where people who have committed an infraction or so-called crime, or tried to escape, were imprisoned.
Not really.
He was surrounded by other officers.
And I just have a very vague image of how they stood there in their neatly pressed uniforms, and shined boots.
But--
Yes.
Because there were-- we were actually told that, and also there was a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a-- a secretary of the camp who kept all the numbers straight, who took our numbers.
Whereas those who were going to stay, they were just sent outside the-- the barrack.
Yes.
They were sent to the gas chamber.

And my father stayed behind, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was the second or third night after we arrived at this other camp that we knew that our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] parents or brothers and sisters were sent to the gas chamber.
And that was July 11.
My mother sent a message that she was in a woman's camp, and later on we heard that they were shipped out of Auschwitz.
That. we didn't know where.
